questions.question: Explain the concept of Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) and discuss three different factor categories used in MFA implementations with an example of each.
keyPoints: MFA requires two or more verification factors: something you know, have, or are
Knowledge factor: password, PIN, security questions
Possession factor: smart card, hardware token, mobile phone
Inherence factor: fingerprint, facial recognition, iris scan

questions.question: Describe the process of password hashing and salting. Why are rainbow tables ineffective against properly salted passwords?
keyPoints: Hashing converts password to fixed-length string using one-way cryptographic function
Salt is random data added to password before hashing to ensure unique hashes
Rainbow tables are pre-computed hash lookup tables for common passwords
Unique salts per user make rainbow table attacks impractical as separate table needed for each salt

questions.question: Discuss the Kerberos authentication protocol in detail, explaining its working principle, the role of Key Distribution Center (KDC), Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT), and session keys. Also mention its advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Kerberos uses symmetric cryptography and trusted third-party authentication service
KDC consists of Authentication Server (AS) and Ticket Granting Server (TGS)
Client obtains TGT from AS, then requests service tickets from TGS using TGT
Advantages: single sign-on, mutual authentication, ticket expiration; Limitations: clock synchronization required, vulnerable to password guessing, KDC is single point of failure

questions.question: Compare and contrast OAuth 2.0 and SAML 2.0 authentication frameworks. Also explain how digital certificates work in certificate-based authentication and their role in establishing trust in authentication systems.
keyPoints: OAuth 2.0 is authorization framework using access tokens, REST-based, ideal for API authentication; SAML 2.0 is XML-based SSO protocol, uses assertions, enterprise SSO scenarios
Digital certificates bind public key to entity identity, signed by Certificate Authority (CA)
Certificate-based auth uses PKI with X.509 certificates, client presents certificate, server verifies signature and validity
Both provide federated identity but differ in use cases, complexity, and protocol design
